commented_code:
```typescript
 function hasChanges(current: string[], original: string[]) {
-  // Always sorts and joins, even when lengths differ
-  return current.sort().join() !== original.sort().join()
+  // Always sorts and stringifies, even when lengths differ
+  return JSON.stringify(current.sort()) !== JSON.stringify(original.sort())

commented_code:
This new approach is more efficient because:
 - It avoids the overhead of sorting and joining the arrays when lengths differ
+- It avoids O(n log n) sorting operations by creating and comparing maps of the element counts, an O(n) procedure

discussion_comments.comment_body: I don’t think this new approach is strictly O(n), technically map access is O(log n) so it’s still the same as the sorting approach. But in practice this can be faster.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
AFAIK, at least in V8, map access cannot be O(log n) - there are only hash calculations, array lookups, and linked list sequential scans. At least while the map fits in memory, and there is no swapping in the operating system.

```
Operation Average Worst
Get       O(1)    O(n) in case of hash collision
Set       O(1)    O(n) in case of hash collision or rehashing while growing the internal arrays
Delete    O(1)    O(n) in case of hash collision or rehashing while shrinking the internal arrays
```

https://itnext.io/v8-deep-dives-understanding-map-internals-45eb94a183df
